brand.title: ESource AI Group (Production)
brand.subtitle: Knowledge Check — CTFS for Account Executives: From Discovery to Deal

questions.question: According to the course, what are the five financial functions that CTFS unifies into one platform?
choices: Budgeting, contracting, forecasting, site payments, and participant payments
Budgeting, invoicing, forecasting, site payments, and auditing
Contracting, forecasting, payments, reporting, and compliance
Budgeting, contracting, payments, analytics, and risk management
questions.feedback_correct: Exactly right—these five functions running on shared data is the core CTFS differentiator.
questions.feedback_incorrect: The correct answer includes budgeting, contracting, forecasting, site payments, and participant payments. Look at Slide 5 for the one-sentence definition of what CTFS unifies.

questions.question: What is the proof point you should use when a prospect says their current process is working fine?
choices: 90% of the top 20 pharma companies use our technology
49% growth in trial sites since 2019, 60% increase in protocol amendments, and $55K per day in late-stage trial delay costs
510,000+ budgets delivered across 200+ geographies
$15B+ in payments processed globally
questions.feedback_correct: These three statistics are your urgency anchors to reframe the problem when a prospect claims their process is fine.
questions.feedback_incorrect: The question asks for proof points that create urgency when a prospect says their process is working. Slide 4 identifies three specific statistics designed for this reframing moment.

questions.question: Which persona is most concerned with high maintenance costs and the risk of adding tools that don't integrate with existing systems?
choices: Gilbert, the Clinical Operations Director
Reena, the VP IT Clinical
Andres, the Digital Transformation Leader
Rachel, the Site Operations Director
questions.feedback_correct: Reena's core pain is managing a complex, regulated tech stack with high maintenance costs and limited integration scalability.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Reena is the VP IT Clinical whose pain centers on tech stack complexity and integration risk. Review the persona cards on Slides 10-13 to match each buyer's distinct pain and vocabulary.

questions.question: What is the key difference between GreenPhire and CTFS, according to the competitive framing in this course?
choices: GreenPhire is less expensive and works with more EDC systems
CTFS covers the entire financial lifecycle from budgeting through participant payments with shared data, while GreenPhire is a point solution for payments only
GreenPhire is designed for smaller trials while CTFS handles large Phase III studies
CTFS integrates with Medidata while GreenPhire does not
questions.feedback_correct: That's the scope distinction—GreenPhire is a payment point solution, while CTFS provides end-to-end financial lifecycle management on shared data.
questions.feedback_incorrect: The key competitive difference is scope: GreenPhire handles payments only, while CTFS covers the entire financial lifecycle (budgeting, contracting, forecasting, site and participant payments) with unified data. See Slide 20 for the full competitive framing.

questions.question: Rachel, the Site Operations Director, has three main value drivers for choosing CTFS—which of these is NOT one of them?
choices: Automated invoice creation mapped to CTA terms
Real-time payment visibility through a portal
AI-driven predictive staffing models for site teams
Fewer disputes and faster payment cycle times
questions.feedback_correct: Rachel's value drivers are around payment predictability and automation—not staffing models. You correctly identified what Rachel actually cares about.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Rachel's pain is cash flow unpredictability and manual invoicing. Her value drivers are automated invoice creation, real-time payment visibility, and faster cycle times—not AI staffing models. Review Slide 13 for Rachel's specific drivers.

questions.question: When you have multiple personas in the room—say, both Gilbert and Reena—how should you structure your opening?
choices: Start with Gilbert's platform benefits, then layer in Reena's architecture requirements
Ask each person to introduce themselves and what they hope to get out of the meeting
Open with the shared pain (fragmentation and cost of delay), then branch to individual value drivers as the conversation develops
Focus on the most senior person in the room first, then address the others
questions.feedback_correct: Starting with shared pain creates alignment, then you pivot to individual drivers based on who each buyer is—this keeps everyone engaged.
questions.feedback_incorrect: When multiple personas are present, Slide 15 recommends opening with shared pain that every buyer feels (fragmentation and delay costs), then branching to individual value drivers as the conversation develops. This approach keeps the whole room aligned before customizing the message.

questions.question: What is the Total Net Value formula that you should use to build a prospect's quantified value proposition?
choices: Total Net Value = (Revenue Uplift + Cost Savings) / Solution Cost
Total Net Value = Revenue Uplift + Cost Savings - Solution Cost
Total Net Value = (Revenue Uplift - Solution Cost) + Cost Savings
Total Net Value = Revenue Uplift - (Cost Savings + Solution Cost)
questions.feedback_correct: That's the three-pillar formula—add the financial gains and subtract the cost of the solution to get the net value the prospect can defend internally.
questions.feedback_incorrect: The formula is: Total Net Value = Revenue Uplift + Cost Savings - Solution Cost. The three pillars are revenue uplift (from faster trials), cost reduction (from fewer manual hours), minus the cost of CTFS itself. See Slide 18 for the breakdown.

questions.question: Andres, the Digital Transformation Leader, is skeptical because his last AI pilot stalled before reaching enterprise scale. How should you counter this objection?
choices: Acknowledge the failure and explain that our AI agents are better trained than his vendor's were
Ask if that pilot had an enterprise orchestration layer connecting it to existing systems, then position CTFS as the infrastructure that makes pilots scalable
Suggest that his organization wasn't ready for AI and offer consulting services to prepare
Recommend starting with a smaller use case before attempting enterprise deployment
questions.feedback_correct: This response addresses the root cause of his failed pilot—lack of infrastructure—and positions CTFS as the fix, not another pilot.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Andres's stalled POC objection requires you to diagnose why it failed, not just offer a better pilot. Slide 25 shows you should ask if the pilot had an orchestration layer, then explain that CTFS is the infrastructure that makes pilots work at enterprise scale.

questions.question: You are in a discovery call and need to map the buying committee to personas. Which three questions should you ask first to identify who is in the room?
choices: What is your budget, timeline, and current vendor?
Who owns financial systems, who signs off on technology decisions, and who represents site operations?
What are your top three pain points, and how are you solving them today?
How many trials are you running, and what trial phases are most important?
questions.feedback_correct: Those three questions map directly to your buyer personas and tell you whose message to lead with—this is discovery priority one.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Slide 24 identifies the three discovery priority questions that help you map the buying committee to personas: who owns financial systems, who signs off on tech decisions, and who represents site operations. Ask these before choosing your message track.

questions.question: What is the value of positioning the Q4 2025 sandbox as the next step for Andres, the stalled-POC buyer?
choices: It gives him time to secure budget approval before a full rollout
It proves enterprise infrastructure works at scale, not another isolated pilot
It allows your implementation team to learn his systems before general availability
It reduces his risk by keeping the trial data separate from production
questions.feedback_correct: For Andres, the sandbox is positioned as proof of enterprise infrastructure—solving the orchestration gap that killed his last pilot.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Slide 26 shows that for Andres specifically, the sandbox is framed as a pilot that proves enterprise scale—addressing the exact reason his last initiative stalled. The sandbox value prop changes by persona; for Andres it's about infrastructure, not budget or risk reduction.
